metadata.title: Accelerated Decline in Hepatic Glucose Production During Fasting in Normal Women Compared With Men
metadata.study_design: Comparative metabolic study with sequential 3-3H-glucose infusions after 14 and 64-hour fasts
metadata.demographics: Five normal men and five normal women in the midfollicular phase of menstrual cycle, matched for age and body mass index, with no history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, kidney disease, or medication use

evidence.quote: five normal men and five normal women underwent sequentiel 3-3H-glucose infusions after both a 14 and a 64hour fast
women exhibit a significantly greater fall in Ra after 64 hours of fasting, which may account for the reportedly greater hypoglycemia during fasting in women

Ten normal volunteers were admitted to the General Clinical Research Center (GCRC) at the Medical College of Virginia for the metabolic studies. Five of the subjects were male and five were female in the midfollicular phase of their menstrual cycle. None had a history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, or kidney disease or were on any medications.
five normal men and five normal women underwent sequentiel S3H-glucose infusions

methods_participants.label: Glucose Tracer Assay - Continuous Infusion Rate

evidence.quote: A primed (25 µCi) continuous (0.25 µCi) infusion of 3-3H-glucose (New England Nuclear, Boston) was administered via a forearm vein from 7 AM until 10 AM
blood samples were obtained at 20-minute intervals during the last hour of the study (9 AM to 10 AM)
Plasma glucose was measured immediately after centrifugation by the glucose oxidase method (Beckman, Fullerton, CA)
Plasma insulin, C-peptide, growth hormone, and glucagon were determined using double-antibody radioimmunoassays
Body composition was assessed by skinfold measurements and impedance plethysmography (BIA-101 Body Composition Analyzer, RJL Systems, Detroit)

methods_participants.label: Inclusion Criteria - Medical History
methods_participants.value: no history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, or kidney disease

evidence.quote: None had a history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, or kidney disease or were on any medications

methods_participants.label: Demographic - Mean Body Fat Percent Females

narrative_results.statement: Plasma glucose values fell by 24% from the 16th to the 64th hour of fasting in both men and women

evidence.quote: Plasma glucose values fell by 24% from the 16th to the 64th hour of fasting in both men (5.23 + 0.03 v 3.96 + 0.14 mmol/L, P < .OOl) and women (4.84 + 0.14 v 3.65 f 0.25 mmol/L, P < .OOl).

narrative_results.statement: Glucose clearance rates did not change significantly in either sex after the 64-hour fast

evidence.quote: Glucose clearance rates did not change significantly in either sex after the 64-hour fast

narrative_results.statement: Changes in plasma glucose were associated with decreases in plasma insulin and C-peptide as well as increases in plasma glucagon

evidence.quote: Changes in plasma glucose were associated with decreases in plasma insulin and C-peptide as well as increases in plasma glucagon (Table 2).

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 describes study population (5 normal women), Pass 2 describes fasting duration (14 and 64 hours). These are different data types being compared.
Pass 1 describes continuous infusion rate (0.25 µCi), Pass 2 describes infusion timing (7 AM to 10 AM). These are different parameters being compared.
Pass 1 describes only skinfold measurements; pass 2 describes both skinfold measurements AND impedance plethysmography (BIA-101), representing different body composition assessment methods
Pass 1 states 5 normal men only; Pass 2 states 5 men and 5 women. Incompatible participant composition.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: direct radioimmunoassay (Gammacoat assay, Clinical Assays, Cambridge, MA)
Plasma glucose values fell by 24% from the 16th to the 64th hour of fasting in both men (5.23 + 0.03 v 3.96 + 0.14 mmol/L, P < .OOl) and women (4.84 + 0.14 v 3.65 f 0.25 mmol/L, P < .OOl).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Rates of glucose appearance (Ra) fell significantly in both men and women after the 64-hour fast
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Rates of glucose appearance (Ra) fell significantly in both men and women during the 64-hour fast

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Both the absolute (0.55 + 0.06 v 0.32 * 0.06 mg.kg-'.min-') and the percent fall in Ra (24.6% + 1.4% v 15.8% t 3.0%, Fig 1, inset) were significantly greater in women compared with men (P -e .05).
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Both the absolute (0.55 + 0.06 v 0.32 * 0.06 mg.kg-'.min-') and the percent fall in Ra (24.6% + 1.4% v 15.8% t 3.0%, Fig 1, inset) were significantly greater in women compared with men (P -e .05)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: The percent fall in Ra was significantly greater in women compared with men
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The fall in Ra was significantly greater in women compared with men

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Changes in plasma glucose were associated with decreases in plasma insulin and C-peptide as well as increases in plasma glucagon (Table 2).
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: plasma glycerol levels were significantly greater in women compared with men after the 64-hour fast (P < .05)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Changes in plasma glucose were associated with decreases in plasma insulin and C-peptide as well as increases in plasma glucagon
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma glycerol levels were significantly greater in women compared with men after the 64-hour fast

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: plasma glycerol levels were significantly greater in women compared with men after the 64-hour fast (P < .05).
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma FFA also rose significantly in men (0.49 + 0.06 v 0.8 1 f 0.09 mmol/L, P < .Ol) and women (0.47 + 0.04 v 0.89 2 0.04 mmol/L, P -c .05), without significant intersex differences

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma glycerol levels were significantly greater in women compared with men after the 64-hour fast
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma FFA rose significantly in both men and women without significant intersex differences

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma FFA also rose significantly in men (0.49 + 0.06 v 0.8 1 f 0.09 mmol/L, P < .Ol) and women (0.47 + 0.04 v 0.89 2 0.04 mmol/L, P -c .05), without significant intersex differences.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: all subjects exhibited at least a sixfold increase during the fast

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Plasma FFA rose significantly in both men and women without significant intersex differences
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: All subjects exhibited at least a sixfold increase in plasma β-hydroxybutyrate levels during the fast

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: In conclusion, 64-hour fasted women exhibit a greater fall in HGO than do men similarly fasted.

semantic_groups.rationale: Pass 1 describes study population (5 normal women), Pass 2 describes fasting duration (14 and 64 hours). These are different data types being compared.
Pass 1 contains fasting duration first assessment (14 hours), Pass 2 is empty. Only one side present.
Pass 1 contains fasting duration second assessment (64 hours), Pass 2 is empty. Only one side present.

Both describe glucose tracer infusion method using 3-3H-glucose. Pass 1 specifies priming dose (25 µCi), Pass 2 describes method as primed continuous infusion. Same underlying fact with different emphasis.
Pass 1 describes continuous infusion rate (0.25 µCi), Pass 2 describes infusion timing (7 AM to 10 AM). These are different parameters being compared.
Pass 1 contains glucose tracer infusion duration (3 hours), Pass 2 is empty. Only one side present.
Both describe blood sampling at 20-minute intervals. Pass 2 adds temporal context (9 AM to 10 AM) but the core fact is the same.
Both describe plasma glucose measurement using glucose oxidase method. Pass 2 adds manufacturer details (Beckman, Fullerton, CA) but describes the same method.
Both describe insulin measurement via double-antibody radioimmunoassay; only differ in label phrasing ('Plasma Insulin Measurement' vs 'Insulin measurement method')
Pass 1 describes plasma C-peptide measurement method; pass 2 is empty/missing
Pass 1 describes plasma glucagon measurement method; pass 2 is empty/missing
Pass 1 describes plasma growth hormone measurement method; pass 2 is empty/missing
Both describe cortisol measurement via direct radioimmunoassay; pass 1 includes specific assay details (Gammacoat, Clinical Assays, Cambridge, MA) while pass 2 omits them
Both describe free fatty acid measurement via enzymatic methods; only differ in label phrasing ('Plasma FFA Measurement' vs 'Free fatty acid measurement method')
Pass 1 describes only skinfold measurements; pass 2 describes both skinfold measurements AND impedance plethysmography (BIA-101), representing different body composition assessment methods
Pass 1 describes impedance plethysmography as assessment method 2; pass 2 is empty/missing
Both describe exclusion of diabetes mellitus history. Pass 1 includes additional exclusions (hypertension, kidney disease) not mentioned in Pass 2, but the diabetes criterion is equivalent.
Both state participants were not on any medications. Wording differs slightly but conveys identical information.
Both specify midfollicular phase of menstrual cycle. Minor capitalization difference only.
Pass 1 describes fasting compliance assessment method; Pass 2 is empty. Information only in one extraction.
Pass 1 states 5 normal men only; Pass 2 states 5 men and 5 women. Incompatible participant composition.
Pass 2 specifies no hypertension history; Pass 1 is empty. This criterion appears in Pass 1's aligned pair (index 16) but not extracted separately here.
Pass 2 specifies no kidney disease history; Pass 1 is empty. This criterion appears in Pass 1's aligned pair (index 16) but not extracted separately here.
Same fact about Ra falling significantly; 'after' vs 'during' the fast represents negligible temporal phrasing difference
Same fact about sex difference in Ra fall; 'percent fall' vs 'fall' is minor wording variation
Identical statements about glucose clearance rates
Pass 1 contains information about plasma glucose associations; Pass 2 is empty
Identical statements about plasma glycerol sex differences
Identical statements about plasma FFA changes
Identical statements about HGO sex differences in fasted state
Identical statements about plasma glucose decline during fasting
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains a specific factual claim about β-hydroxybutyrate increase during fasting. No comparison possible.
